metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for the `from_env()` config wiring, e.g., a unit test `test_llm_config_overlap_fields` that (a) asserts the two fields exist on `LLMConfig` with correct defaults, and (b) calls `LLMConfig.from_env()` with env vars `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_CONF_THRESHOLD=0.85` and `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_SURFACE_CAP=5` and asserts the constructed instance reflects those values.
findings.finding: The config wiring deliverable (adding `overlap_conf_threshold` and `overlap_surface_cap` to `LLMConfig` and `from_env()`) has no dedicated proving command; the only test that references configurability is `test_judge_precision_recall`, which uses a FakeRunner and never exercises the `from_env()` wiring path.
findings.location: AC5 — 'overlap_conf_threshold (0.7)/overlap_surface_cap (3) configurable on LLMConfig'; Config section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Testing section: `test_llm_config_overlap_fields` — instantiates `LLMConfig` with non-default values and asserts `.overlap_conf_threshold == <value>` and `.overlap_surface_cap == <value>`; also calls `from_env()` with patched env vars and asserts correct field values. Add `::test_llm_config_overlap_fields` to the AC5 proving commands.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a direction-reconciliation rule to the aggregation spec: for directional relations (supersedes, depends_on), require that both orderings agree on direction (i.e., A→B returns 'supersedes'/'depends_on' AND B→A returns 'unrelated'/'related_distinct', or define a canonical tie-break), OR restrict dual-ordering agreement for directional relations to mean 'the A→B ordering returns the relation AND the B→A ordering agrees it is the same relation from the same source perspective'; surface only the A→B call's link_command as the canonical direction.
findings.finding: For directional relations (supersedes, depends_on), when both orderings return the same relation label they produce inverted link_commands ('rebar link A B supersedes' vs 'rebar link B A supersedes'). The plan does not specify which ordering's direction to use when emitting the single surfaced link_command, so the implementer may emit a link in the wrong direction.
findings.location: What — output schema; 'link_command = "rebar link <query> <counterpart> <relation>"' and aggregation rule
findings.suggested_fix: For directional relations, define agreement as: the A→B call returns `relation R` AND the B→A call ALSO returns `relation R` only when it semantically confirms the same directionality (e.g., B→A returns `related_distinct` or `unrelated` for a true A-supersedes-B pair). Alternatively, restrict dual-ordering confirmation of directional relations to: the A→B result is surfaceable AND the B→A result is `related_distinct` or the same relation with matching `shared_artifact`; always use A→B's link_command as canonical.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the maximum K (stage-1 candidate count passed to the judge), whether calls are sequential or parallelised, and how rate limits are handled (e.g., semaphore/token-bucket) so that latency and cost are bounded.
findings.finding: The plan specifies '2K judge calls (both orderings)' but never bounds K (the number of stage-1 candidates passed to the judge), and provides no concurrency model, rate-limit handling, or parallelism strategy for these LLM calls.
findings.location: What — output schema / 'K candidates → 2K judge calls (both orderings)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `overlap_max_candidates: int = 10` cap on `LLMConfig` (enforced before the judge loop), document that calls are sequential by default, and note that a `max_concurrency` knob (e.g., `asyncio.Semaphore`) can be introduced in S7 wiring if latency becomes a concern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between 'prompt body MUST encode the both-orderings instruction' and the 2K-separate-single-ordering-calls architecture: either (a) restate the prompt requirement as 'the prompt instructs the model to classify THIS ordering only, and acknowledges it will be called twice with inverted inputs,' or (b) clarify that 'both-orderings instruction' refers to the judge.py orchestration layer, not the prompt body.
findings.finding: The prompt body for `overlap_judge.md` is required to encode 'the both-orderings instruction', but each call to the judge is a single-turn call over ONE ordering (A→B or B→A). Instructing an LLM in a single-turn prompt to 'consider both orderings' when the prompt only contains one ordering's data is a pink-elephant antipattern: the instruction names something the model cannot see, which can cause confabulation or the model attempting to imagine the reverse ordering.
findings.location: Scope section — `overlap_judge.md` body requirements; What section — '2K judge calls (both orderings)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify what 'both-orderings instruction' means in the prompt: rename it (e.g. 'single-ordering instruction with positional context') or add a note that the prompt evaluates ONE ordering per call and the 'instruction' means telling the LLM which direction (A→B or B→A) it is judging, not asking it to evaluate both in one turn.
findings.finding: The Scope section requires the rubric prompt body to encode 'the both-orderings instruction', but the architecture described in the What section is 2K separate single-turn calls (one per ordering) orchestrated by judge.py — the prompt should only need to evaluate a single A→B ordering per call. An implementer could read 'both-orderings instruction' as a directive for the prompt to ask the LLM to judge both A→B and B→A in a single response, contradicting the 2K-call design and the single_turn execution_mode.
findings.location: Scope section — 'rubric prompt … whose body MUST encode: … the both-orderings instruction'; What section — '2K judge calls (both orderings), flat in corpus size'; execution_mode: single_turn
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the both-orderings instruction' with 'an ordering-direction context instruction (the prompt receives and evaluates exactly one ordering — A→B — per call; judge.py issues the symmetric B→A call separately)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the rubric prompt body should encode only the per-call classification instructions (CLOSED relation set, require shared_artifact, abstain-when-unsure); move the 'both-orderings instruction' note to the judge.py spec section where it belongs as an orchestration rule, not a prompt-body requirement.
findings.finding: The rubric prompt (overlap_judge.md, execution_mode: single_turn) is mandated to encode 'the both-orderings instruction', but each prompt invocation sees exactly one ordered pair (A, B); the both-orderings mechanism is an orchestration concern implemented entirely in judge.py (2K separate calls). Encoding 'both-orderings instruction' inside the single-turn per-call prompt conflates two distinct concerns and will either produce dead/ignored prompt text or misguide the LLM into attempting something it cannot do in a single turn.
findings.location: Scope section — 'rubric prompt src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md ... whose body MUST encode: ... the both-orderings instruction'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the both-orderings instruction' in the prompt-body MUST list with a note that judge.py orchestrates two calls and aggregates; the prompt itself should only say 'classify this single ordered pair (A→B)' — the LLM need not know it is being called twice.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the 'both-orderings instruction' requirement in the prompt spec: state whether it means (a) the prompt tells the LLM to be ordering-aware (contextual framing only, no dual output) while judge.py still makes two separate calls, or (b) something else — and reconcile this with the '2K calls' architecture statement.
findings.finding: The rubric prompt content requirement says it MUST encode 'the both-orderings instruction,' but the architecture already handles both orderings externally (judge.py makes 2K calls, one per ordering). Encoding a 'both-orderings instruction' inside the prompt conflicts with the dual-call design and could cause an executor to implement redundant/contradictory logic.
findings.location: Scope section — 'rubric prompt … whose body MUST encode: … the both-orderings instruction'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the both-orderings instruction' with 'a note that the LLM is evaluating one ordered pair (A, B) and should not infer from ordering which item is more important — judge.py issues a second call with (B, A) and aggregates externally.' This keeps the prompt single-ordering and the dual-call logic unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan (or split `test_abstain_dropped` into two sub-cases) that the test covers the asymmetric scenario: one ordering abstains and the other returns a surfaceable verdict — asserting no slot is consumed.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'abstain: true in EITHER ordering downgrades' but it is ambiguous whether `test_abstain_dropped` covers the asymmetric case (one ordering abstains, the other returns a valid surfaceable verdict) or only the symmetric case (both abstain). The asymmetric case is the more dangerous edge: a single-ordering abstain must still suppress surfacing.
findings.location: Testing section — `test_abstain_dropped`; What section: 'An LLM-emitted abstain: true in EITHER ordering also downgrades (not surfaced) and does not consume a surface slot'
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the test to `test_abstain_either_ordering_dropped` and document that it tests both (a) both abstain and (b) only one ordering abstains but the other is surfaceable, asserting neither case surfaces a finding.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `overlap_verdict` contract builder in `contracts.py` (or `judge.py`) must include a docstring/comment encoding the dual-ordering aggregation rule (both agree + artifact non-null + both conf ≥ threshold → surface; else downgrade), so the decision rule is co-located with the schema it governs.
findings.finding: The dual-ordering aggregation protocol (both orderings must agree on the same surfaceable relation AND each cite a non-null `shared_artifact` AND both `confidence >= overlap_conf_threshold`) is specified only in the 'What' narrative section and is not specified to be co-located with the `overlap_verdict` schema definition or the `register_contract` builder in `contracts.py`. An implementer reading only the schema/contract would not know the two-verdict aggregation rule that must be applied when consuming this schema.
findings.location: What — output schema section (aggregation rule); Scope — `contracts.py` registration

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a self-contained implementation spec for `_llm_float` directly in this story (the four-line precedence chain + parse-failure fallthrough, mirroring `_llm_int`'s `bool`-guard and `try/except` pattern) so the story is implementable regardless of whether `wood-heedless-sheldrake` has landed.
findings.finding: `_llm_float` does not exist anywhere in the codebase, yet the plan makes a confident assertion that sibling story `wood-heedless-sheldrake` 'adds' it. The plan's fallback clause ('if S2 has not landed, this story adds it, mirroring `_llm_int`') provides only a by-analogy spec — no implementation details for the float coercion logic (e.g. env-var parsing, CLI override parsing, fall-through on non-float input). If the sibling has not landed when this story is implemented, the implementer must infer the full `_llm_float` body from `_llm_int`, which is reasonable but leaves an unbacked claim at the center of the config wiring.
findings.location: Config section — `from_env` wiring paragraph: '`_llm_float` is added by S2 wood-heedless-sheldrake, a sibling — if S2 has not landed in the stack yet, this story adds it, mirroring `_llm_int`.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Config section: '`_llm_float(table, cli, env_name, file_key, default: float) -> float`: same CLI>env>file>default precedence as `_llm_int`; skip `bool` values (`isinstance(fv, bool)` guard); parse via `float(str(c).strip())`, fall through on `ValueError/TypeError`; return `default` if all candidates fail.' This is the exact analog of the existing `_llm_int` body at config.py:388–405.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `_llm_float` helper's exact contract (fallthrough condition for unparseable values, and whether `0.0` is a valid non-fallthrough value), either inline in this plan or by confirming the sibling story's implementation covers these cases.
findings.finding: `_llm_float` is referenced as the config resolver for `overlap_conf_threshold` but does not exist in `src/rebar/llm/config.py`. The plan acknowledges this (noting it may land from sibling story `wood-heedless-sheldrake`) but specifies this story should add it 'mirroring `_llm_int`' without defining the float-specific fallthrough/precision contract (e.g. what happens on unparseable input, whether `0.0` is treated as a falsy blank-through like `_llm_str` treats empty strings).
findings.location: Config section — `src/rebar/llm/config.py` `from_env()` wiring; `_llm_float` helper
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Config section: '`_llm_float` resolves like `_llm_int` but parses via `float()`; unparseable values fall through to the next layer; `0.0` is a valid value and must NOT trigger fallthrough (unlike `_llm_str`'s blank-string check).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the fixture serialization format and layout in the Scope section (e.g., 'a single `pairs.json` file containing a JSON array of `{a_digest, b_digest, gold_relation}` objects') so the test loader and fixture author make the same assumption.
findings.finding: The labeled fixture format (`{a_digest, b_digest, gold_relation}`) is specified structurally but the file serialization format (JSON, YAML, Python, etc.) and whether the ≥12 pairs are individual files or one collection file is left unstated.
findings.location: Scope section — 'tests/fixtures/overlap_pairs/ with ≥12 labeled pairs (each {a_digest, b_digest, gold_relation})'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the fixture bullet: 'stored as a single `tests/fixtures/overlap_pairs/pairs.json` JSON array; each element is `{"a_digest": "...", "b_digest": "...", "gold_relation": "<relation>"}`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC bullet: 'Schema wiring is complete: `test_every_schema_file_is_wired` passes (covers OVERLAP_VERDICT in schemas/__init__.py and OUTPUT_SCHEMAS["overlap_judge"] entry).'
findings.finding: The Scope section describes wiring `schemas/__init__.py` (adding OVERLAP_VERDICT constant, OUTPUT_SCHEMAS entry, __all__ update) as a deliverable to ensure `test_every_schema_file_is_wired` passes, but no AC bullet lists running that test or asserts this wiring is correct — it is an uncovered deliverable.
findings.location: Scope section — 'WIRE the schema in src/rebar/schemas/__init__.py … so test_every_schema_file_is_wired passes'; Acceptance Criteria section (no corresponding bullet)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to the AC (or an explicit bullet) that if `_llm_float` is not already present in config.py, this story must introduce it, and verify its presence is covered by the `test_agree`/`test_llm_error_abstain` tests (which exercise the configured threshold path) or a dedicated config-loading unit test.
findings.finding: The Config section conditionally requires adding `_llm_float` helper to `src/rebar/llm/config.py` ('if S2 has not landed in the stack yet, this story adds it, mirroring _llm_int'), but no AC bullet covers this conditional deliverable — if it is needed, it will be silently absent from verification.
findings.location: Config section — '_llm_float is added by S2 wood-heedless-sheldrake, a sibling — if S2 has not landed in the stack yet, this story adds it, mirroring _llm_int'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md/design-doc section capturing: (a) why dual-ordering is required (position-bias mitigation), (b) the aggregation rule (both agree + non-null artifact + both conf ≥ threshold → surface), (c) the new `overlap_verdict` contract, and (d) the config surface rationale.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — a bounded dual-ordering LLM pairwise judge as a pipeline stage — along with a new contract (`overlap_verdict`), a new config surface on `LLMConfig`, and a new aggregation rule (position-bias dual-ordering), but specifies no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to capture these decisions.
findings.location: ## Why / ## Scope / ## Config sections
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNNN-overlap-judge-dual-ordering.md` (or append to CLAUDE.md under a 'Stage-2 overlap judge' heading) covering: the precision problem motivation, the dual-ordering invariant, the abstain-downgrades rule, the contract schema, and the threshold defaults.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a calibration note or lightweight spike: run the judge with the real LLM over the ≥12 labeled fixture pairs (even manually) and record observed confidence distributions to justify or adjust the 0.7 default before shipping.
findings.finding: The `overlap_conf_threshold` default of 0.7 is asserted without a measurement plan against a real LLM; the only validation (`test_judge_precision_recall`) uses a FakeRunner with canned verdicts that the author controls, so it validates aggregation logic but not whether 0.7 is the correct threshold calibration for the real model.
findings.location: ## Config section / ## Testing section — test_judge_precision_recall
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-time calibration step (can be a script in `scripts/` or a note in the ADR): sample the labeled fixture pairs through the real LLM runner, plot/log confidence histograms per relation class, and pick the threshold at the precision/recall operating point. Document the result as the justification for the default.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one retry with exponential backoff (e.g., up to 2 retries) before promoting an LLM error to abstain, and emit a structured warning/metric when the abstain-on-error path is taken so operators can distinguish transient degradation from genuine 'no overlap' results.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no retry or backoff before treating an LLM error as abstain; transient failures (network blip, rate-limit 429) silently convert valid candidates into abstains, degrading recall with only a log entry as signal.
findings.location: What — output schema / 'Errors: LLM timeout/error/malformed output on a candidate → treated as abstain (dropped), logged'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the per-ordering LLM call in a small retry loop (e.g., `tenacity.retry(stop=stop_after_attempt(2), wait=wait_exponential(...))`); on exhaustion log a WARNING with candidate id and error type, and optionally increment a counter metric `overlap_judge.abstain_on_error` so the rate is observable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named unit test (e.g. `test_confidence_below_threshold_downgrade`) that covers the case where both orderings agree on relation and artifact but one ordering's confidence is below `overlap_conf_threshold`, asserting the result is NOT surfaced.
findings.finding: The aggregation rule has a third failure mode — one ordering passes the confidence threshold, the other does not — that is not covered by any named test. The plan specifies 'both confidence >= overlap_conf_threshold' is required to surface, but only `test_disagree_downgrade` (relation mismatch) and `test_abstain_dropped` (LLM abstain) cover non-surface paths; a case where relations agree but one confidence is below threshold is absent.
findings.location: Testing section — named tests `test_agree`, `test_disagree_downgrade`; AC line 'Judges both orderings, aggregates by the stated rule'
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_overlap_judge.py`, add a FakeRunner fixture returning two `overlap_verdict` responses with identical relation/artifact but `confidence=[0.85, 0.62]` (threshold=0.7) and assert no finding is surfaced; add the test name to the AC list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `_llm_float` has ≥3 total call-sites across the stack (this story + sibling) before authoring it as a shared helper; if this is the only float config knob in the current milestone, inline the `_llm_int`-style resolution directly for `overlap_conf_threshold` and defer the helper until a second consumer exists.
findings.finding: `_llm_float` helper would have exactly one call-site if introduced by this story, violating Rule-of-Three. The plan notes sibling story 'wood-heedless-sheldrake' may already add it; if that story has NOT landed, this story authors a single-use helper with no other consumers in the current done-definition.
findings.location: Config section: '`_llm_float` is added by S2 wood-heedless-sheldrake, a sibling — if S2 has not landed in the stack yet, this story adds it, mirroring `_llm_int`.'
findings.suggested_fix: In `from_env`, resolve `overlap_conf_threshold` inline (mirroring `_llm_int`'s logic for float: `float(str(c).strip())`) rather than via a new `_llm_float` function, unless wood-heedless-sheldrake confirms it adds ≥2 more float knobs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` (updating `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS` with `'_llm_float': (2, '')`) and `docs/env-vars.md` (regeneration) to the plan's edit targets alongside `src/rebar/llm/config.py`.
findings.finding: The plan names `src/rebar/llm/config.py` as an edit target for adding `_llm_float` (a new env-resolution helper), but does not name `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` as a required edit. That script's `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS` dict (line 43) currently lists `_llm_int` so the scanner resolves calls like `_llm_int(table, cli, "REBAR_LLM_...", ...)` to their env-var names. Without adding `_llm_float` to that dict, calls `_llm_float(table, cli, "REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_CONF_THRESHOLD", ...)` will be invisible to the scanner, `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_CONF_THRESHOLD` will be absent from the generated `docs/env-vars.md`, and `test_check_mode_clean_against_committed_tree` (tests/unit/test_gen_env_registry.py:59) will fail.
findings.location: Config section / `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS
findings.suggested_fix: In the Config section, add: 'Also edit `scripts/gen_env_registry.py`: add `"_llm_float": (2, "")` to `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS` (mirroring the existing `_llm_int` entry), then regenerate `docs/env-vars.md` by running `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_config_llm.py` to the plan's named edit targets and note that the `_clean` fixture's `monkeypatch.delenv` list must be extended with `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_CONF_THRESHOLD` and `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_SURFACE_CAP`.
findings.finding: The `tests/unit/test_config_llm.py` `_clean` fixture has a hard-coded list of env var names it `monkeypatch.delenv`s before each test (lines 29-43). It currently lists `REBAR_LLM_MODEL`, `REBAR_LLM_MAX_TOKENS`, etc. but does not include `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_CONF_THRESHOLD` or `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_SURFACE_CAP`. When this story adds those fields to `LLMConfig.from_env`, any test that sets those env vars and does not restore them could bleed into other tests in the same module. The plan doesn't name `test_config_llm.py` as a consumer requiring update.
findings.location: Config section / `tests/unit/test_config_llm.py` _clean fixture

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `category: overlap` does not conflict with any existing prompt-hygiene or prompt-index test that enumerates or validates the closed category set; if `overlap` is a new routing category, document it in the prompt-library's category taxonomy or use the existing `plan-review-pass` if that better fits the routing intent.
findings.finding: The plan declares `category: overlap` in the new rubric prompt's front-matter. The existing prompt category values form a de-facto closed set (`review`, `code-review-pass`, `plan-review-criterion`, `plan-review-pass`); tests like `test_reviewer_prompt_hygiene` and `test_prompt_index` may validate or enumerate categories. A novel `overlap` value is not checked for compatibility with the prompt-index test infrastructure.
findings.location: Scope section: 'rubric prompt `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md` (`execution_mode: single_turn`, `outputs: overlap_verdict`, `category: overlap`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `overlap_judge.md` must be registered in `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json` (with a new `category: overlap` entry) or whether the `category` field is purely informational — and add the registration step to Scope if required.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `category: overlap` as a new reviewer prompt front-matter category, but no such category exists in the codebase (`index.json` has categories like `review`, `plan-review-pass`, etc.), and the plan does not specify whether the `overlap_judge` reviewer must be registered in `index.json` or whether the `category` field is purely informational metadata with no dispatch consequences. If the reviewer-index loader or category-dispatch code consumes this field, an unregistered `category: overlap` will either silently be ignored or fail.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md` (`category: overlap`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief prior-art note to the Why or What section explaining why the dual-ordering LLM approach was chosen over a single-call dual-ordering prompt or a lightweight NLI/cross-encoder model (cost, latency, or capability tradeoff).
findings.finding: The plan does not compare the chosen dual-ordering LLM pairwise judge against the established alternative of a single-call judge with both orderings in one prompt (the 'calibration in a single call' variant used in many LLM-as-judge papers), nor against lighter-weight options such as an NLI/cross-encoder model (e.g. `cross-encoder/nli-deberta-v3-base`) which would avoid 2K LLM calls entirely. The dual-ordering approach IS justified on position-bias grounds, but no consideration of cost/latency tradeoffs vs. these alternatives is present.
findings.location: Why section and What section — 'K candidates → 2K judge calls (both orderings), flat in corpus size'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define or justify `category: overlap` as a valid reviewer prompt front-matter category value — either add it to a known category (e.g. `category: overlap-judge`) with a specified routing mechanism, or document that `category` is a free-form label and confirm the loader accepts arbitrary values without a registry entry.
findings.finding: `category: overlap` is a new reviewer prompt front-matter category value that does not exist in the codebase taxonomy. All existing reviewer prompts use `review`, `plan-review-criterion`, `plan-review-pass`, or `code-review-pass`; `index.json` only registers reviewers under `review`. The plan never specifies where this new category is registered, how the prompt-loader routes it, or whether a new `index.json` entry is required for `overlap_judge`.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md` front-matter `category: overlap`
findings.suggested_fix: Change `category: overlap` to an existing category such as `category: review` (since the overlap judge is not a plan-review-criterion or code-review-pass), or specify that the new `overlap` category is registered in a new `overlap/index.json` with an explicit entry for `overlap_judge`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
